titleStmt.title: A Transcription of John in Codex Fossatensis
VL9A
The New Testament
The Gospel according to John
john
titleStmt.funder: The Arts and Humanities Research Council

sponsor.name: The Institute for Textual Scholarship and Electronic Editing

publisher.name: The Institute for Textual Scholarship and Electronic Editing
University of Birmingham

availability.p: Licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0): available for re-use provided attribution is made to the original creators (the IGNTP and any named participants) and an indication is given of any changes made. See further .

msItem.title: Quattuor Euangelia
Four Gospels in order Mt Mc Lc Jo

handDesc.p: Written in majuscule. The copyist occasionally writes the final line of a column in Anglo-Saxon minuscule.

decoDesc.p: Black ink, with rubrics and decoration in blue, yellow and red.

origin.origPlace: Northumbria most probable, although Kent has also been suggested.

surrogates.bibl: Colour digital images on CD published by NLR in 2001.
Six of these are reproduced at http://arts-itsee.bham.ac.uk/AnaServer?verbum+0+start.anv+ms=9A

projectDesc.p: This transcription was made by the Verbum Project as part of an online and printed edition of the Old Latin versions of John in 2009.

editorialDecl.p: This transcription covers ff. 177r-213v. The manuscript includes capitula before the gospel (Type C). Corrections towards the Vulgate were made at an early period, probably in the scriptorium. Blank space at the end of the line is sometimes used for the completion of the line below. Quotations are marked with a diple.The transcription includes foliation, lineation, all punctuation and abbreviations. The initial transcription file was a plain text file for use with COLLATE. Further information about the procedures followed may be found in the electronic edition at
This second edition of the file was converted to XML by Catherine Smith using the project's Python conversion scripts 1.0.
The XML specifications used are version 1.5 of the IGNTP Guidelines available at

revisionDesc.change: Adjustment of transcription to version 1.5 of the XML by Hugh Houghton.
Reconversion of the original plain text file using Python scripts to version 1.4 of the XML by Catherine Smith and manual checking.
Hyphen entity replaced by lb attribute break="no" by Hugh Houghton
Changes made in order to display properly with VLI.xsl transformation by Hugh Houghton
Transcription converted to the TEI XML schema and corrected for parsing by Hugh Houghton

ab.note: Erasure verified from MS, although the first hand is illegible.
Chapter numbers occur in the left hand margin of the line above.
The last 3 letters 'nis' project into the margin, but still seem to be first hand.
Spacing suggests that the first hand may have written 'uinum', but no correction is visible.

No correction is visible here in the gap at the end of the line in the MS; however, the spacing of the first character on the line below suggests that it was added later, and the word may originally have been split e/o.
The word division has been altered to pere-at.
The letter shapes of the end of this line are unusual.
No erasure is visible on this line, so aeternam appears to be an addition rather than replacement.
The ligatures at the end of the line are unusual, but appear to be by the first hand.
doesn 't seem to be an erasure
The first hand may have written 'chanaan'.
The erasure has been confirmed from the manuscript.
The erasure under 'super' has been confirmed from the manuscript.
The erasure has been confirmed from the manuscript.
Quire signature XIII in bottom margin.
'aeternam' may be a correction, as the stylus seems narrower, but the ink colour and letter forms are first hand. Cf. 'semetipso' on the bottom line.
The corrector appears to have changed the division of the word to 'iudaeo-rum'. This is followed by an erasure of a line and a half.
corr but illeg; eo is corr, rum looks fh
There is no erasure here: confirmed from MS
correction but illegible; beware u on verso
checked in ms, no rasura under fragmenta
'a' appears to be first hand, with no erasure.
For the first hand reading, compare the other instances of 'manna' in this ms.
MS shows -es is correction; original may have been omne¬
For the first hand reading, compare the other instances of 'manna' in this ms.
The first hand might have written 'esca ÷', but this is not legible.
'patres uestri' may in part be a correction - perhaps from parentes?
No erasure can be detected after 'ei'.
An erasure is not obvious here, but the space suggests that four characters (such as uere?) might have been erased.
the last a of -abat is a very red rubric, redder thatn rest, ihs may be fh, maybe not
It is not clear whether 'et dixit' is written over an erasure.
'misit' may have been written over an erasure, but it seems to be first hand.
It is not obvious from the MS whether 'circumcisionis' is written in rasura, but the spacing suggests that it was.
There may be an erasure to the end of the line.
The erasure at the end of this line has been confirmed from the manuscript.
The overline looks as if the first hand may have written manu¬, but the added 's' seems to be the same colour ink on the images.
The bow of q and u have some brown ink suggesting reinforcement.
'autem' appears not to be added in an erasure.
'sunt' cannot confidently be identified as a correction form the MS, so has been recorded as first hand.
It is possible that 'templum' is a correction for 'templo'.
This erasure has been confirmed from the manuscript. It may have read 'de ea' or 'de illa'.
Note that 'ur' in the erasure is from the other side.
The ink colour of the 'o' in 'uenio' suggests that it may be an addition.
The correction is added in the bottom margin, marked by d(eest) and h(ic) signs.
There does not appear to be an erasure in the manuscript at this point.
There is no erasure visible here in the manuscript.
'uos' is written as a ligature, apparently by the first hand (in comparison with other line endings on this column).
Quire signature XIIII at foot of page.
There is no erasure visible here in the manuscript.
Reading confirmed from MS; probably a first hand correction
Although this line projects into the margin it appears to be first hand.
The 'hi' of 'mihi' appear to have been overwritten; the spacing suggests that there may also be an erasure at the end of this addition on the line above.
The 'non' may be a later addition.
The ink colour and lack of internal colouring on 'a' suggest that it may be a correction; if so it was made before the rubricated dots were added around the edge.
There may be an erasure here.
This erasure has been confirmed from the manuscript.
There is a cross (+) above the 'et', possibly to indicate the new Ammonian section.
This erasure has not been verified from the manuscript, but seems likely given the spacing.
The ink colour of 'autem encenia' is different, although the script is first hand.
Investigation of the manuscript suggests that 'pater in me est' may have been written over a rasura by the first hand.
ink looks fh, but shape suggests correction
The correction may be over an erasure.
The erasure has been confirmed from the manuscript. It may have read 'sextum diem'.
s seems first hand in MS
'usque' is underlined. This is not found elsewhere in the MS; its purpose is unclear.
this line seems first hand from MS
Quire signature XU in bottom margin.
Some of the letters on this line may be corrected, but the MS is unclear.
There may be an erasure at the end of this line.
The erasures have been verified from the manuscript: this line appeared to start with a hanging capital.
The use of the signe de renvoi suggests that the three words between the lines are a correction.
Erasure verified from manuscript. The 't' may remain from the original writing.
4-5 characters have been erased after 'patrem', with a further 10 or so characters right-aligned on the line above as a continuation of this line.
The'o' of 'uenio' may be a correction, or simply reinking (as with other letters on this page).
There may be a correction here.
The spacing suggests that there may be an erasure at the end of this line, but nothing is clear on the manuscript.
Ink colour and letter forms suggest 'suo' is an addition, but it is not clear whether it is written over an erasure.
The last five lines of this page appear to be written by a slightly different hand.
Although 'me' has been added after 'misisti', it seems to be accompanied by a signe de renvoi matching one between 'tu' and 'misisti'.
The deletion by underdots is unusual in this manuscript.
The correction is written acroos the top margin of the page.
There may be an erasure at the end of this line.
Quire signature XUI in bottom margin.
The corrector has changed the word division to iudae-orum.
There is a possible erasure of two characters at the end of this line.
There is a cross '+' above the 'd' of 'dicit'.
The size of 'tu scis' indicates that it is a later addition.
There are 8 runic characters incised into the page at the end of this line. The introductory material to the CDROM of images, by A.A. Khlevov, identifies these as an Old English name: Edelthryth.

There are a number of characters inscribed with a blunt point after the end of the gospel, which Kilpio and Kahlas-Tarkka 2001 describe as 11th century: "Iascu... ta in di¬ \ tune dei \ frideger". as well as the library indication 'Ex Museo Petri Dubrowsky'.

This page is blank except for the inscription in a later hand "hab[lig]et[/lig] de ibernaticu¬ ·lv· mod(os) ₇ sextarios ·iu· de tramisiu¬"; according to Kilpio and Kahlas-Tarkka 2001, this is a tenth century hand; they expand mod¬ as 'modios', and give 'I' in place of 'iu'.
